title: After Moche & Nazca
bullets: By 600 CE the brilliant Regional Development cultures were all in trouble: the Moche battered by El Niño floods and droughts, the Nazca undermined by huarango deforestation, the Lima and Recuay quietly contracting on the central coast and northern highlands.

The standard archaeological label for what came next is the Middle Horizon — the period between roughly 600 and 1000 CE when shared Wari and Tiwanaku iconography spread across most of the central Andes for the first time since Chavín.

The Andes are not one landscape but four stacked vertically — yunga, quechua, suni, puna — and the central Andean economic strategy was the vertical archipelago, in which a community maintains colonies in distant ecological zones to access products from every altitude band.
Tiwanaku and Wari scaled the household-level vertical archipelago to imperial size, running the first Andean empires on a stack rather than a map — the road was vertical, not horizontal.

Neither Tiwanaku nor Wari developed a writing system: across four centuries of imperial history not a single ruler from either empire is known by name, and the early Wari khipu records have never been deciphered.

Everything historians know about the Middle Horizon is reconstructed from architecture, ceramics, textiles, burials, ice cores, and sediment cores — there are no inscriptions, no chronicles, and no native histories to consult.

The Tang Chinese were producing Li Bai's poetry on paper while Tiwanaku was carving the Bennett Monolith without a single inscribed character — the Andean states were operating on the same imperial scale as their global contemporaries with a documentary record closer to prehistoric than historic.
subtitle: the regional cultures collapse and the highlands take over

title: Tiwanaku Rising
bullets: Tiwanaku sat at 12,500 feet on the southern shore of Lake Titicaca — more than twice the elevation of Denver, in air with 60% of sea-level oxygen — and grew into a planned city of 20,000 to 40,000 people, one of the highest urban centers ever sustained in human history.

Raised-field agriculture (suka kollu) made the city possible: water in surrounding channels absorbed solar heat by day and released it at night, raising platform temperatures by 2–3°C — exactly the difference between fatal frost and an unharmed potato crop.

When archaeologist Clark Erickson rebuilt the ancient suka kollu system with Aymara communities in the 1980s, potato yields tripled compared to conventional dryland farming — the millennium-dormant Tiwanaku agricultural technology still worked.

The Akapana pyramid was a 60-foot platform with an internal stone drainage system that channeled water through the building in a zigzag pattern, deliberately mimicking the way water flows from mountain peaks down to the altiplano fields.
The Gateway of the Sun is a single 10-ton block of andesite carved with the central Staff God flanked by 48 winged attendants — the most reproduced single image of pre-Inca Andean religious art.

The Pumapunku platform was built from interlocking H-shaped andesite blocks weighing up to 130 tons, cut to fit each other with millimeter tolerances using copper chisels, sand-and-water grinding, and the same patient hand-work that built the Egyptian pyramids and Gothic cathedrals.

The economy ran on llama caravans of 200–500 animals each carrying 60–80 pounds of freeze-dried potatoes (chuño), dried llama meat (charqui), copper, obsidian, and finished textiles across hundreds of miles of altiplano.

Tiwanaku planted colonies in the Moquegua Valley on the Pacific coast, the Cochabamba Valley in modern Bolivia, and the Atacama Desert oases of northern Chile — the vertical archipelago at imperial scale.
subtitle: raised fields, the Gateway of the Sun, and a city at 12,500 feet

title: Wari Founding
bullets: Wari erupted into the Ayacucho basin sometime around 550–600 CE, growing from the local Huarpa village tradition into an urban center of 25,000–40,000 people within a generation or two.

At Conchopata, archaeologists excavated dozens of giant ceramic urns — some over four feet tall — ritually smashed in offering pits and painted with unmistakably Tiwanaku-style Staff God imagery, the smoking gun for Wari's wholesale import of Tiwanaku religious vocabulary.

The signature Wari innovation was the planned rectangular administrative compound — high enclosing walls, internal grid of patio groups, restricted access — replicated identically across hundreds of miles, the work of a single imperial planning office.

Pikillaqta in the Cusco basin contained over 700 rectangular rooms in a rigorous grid of 501 patio groups across 120 acres, built on a template identical to other Wari outposts a thousand miles from the capital.

The wamani provincial system divided Wari territory into administrative districts, each centered on a planned compound, each sending mit'a labor tribute to the central state — exactly the system the Inca would later inherit and run on a much larger scale.

The richest pre-Inca burial assemblage ever recovered in Peru came from the Castillo de Huarmey on the north-central coast, excavated in 2012: an undisturbed mausoleum of 58 high-status women with thousands of luxury offerings.

The Inca are usually presented as the original Andean empire, but the road system, the storehouses, the wamani structure, the mit'a labor tribute, and even the planned-grid city template were all Wari inventions — the Inca were the second iteration, not the first.

The contemporary Tang Chinese were building Chang'an as the largest planned city in the world, and the Wari were doing the same thing on a smaller scale in central Peru, independently, with no contact and no shared antecedent.
subtitle: the planned grid city and the wamani system the Inca later inherited

title: The Two Empires Side by Side
bullets: Tiwanaku and Wari shared the central Andes for roughly four centuries — geographic neighbors, contemporaries with comparable scale and shared religious vocabulary — and we don't know whether they ever fought a single battle.

There are no known Wari-Tiwanaku battles, no defensive fortifications on either frontier facing the other, and no bodies with weapon trauma matching the other empire's weapons across the entire archaeological record.

Wari was a centralized state with planned provincial compounds and paved roads, while Tiwanaku ran a more dispersed colony-based empire connected by llama caravan — bureaucratic versus religious-confederational.

The leading scholarly hypothesis is that Wari spread Quechua and Tiwanaku spread Aymara — the modern linguistic geography of the central Andes is essentially the fossil imprint of the two Middle Horizon empires.

Quechua and Aymara are genuinely separate language families, as different from each other as English is from Greek, which means the Wari and Tiwanaku elites probably could not understand each other's everyday speech even though they shared a religious vocabulary.

Wari pushed north and west into central and northern Peru while Tiwanaku pushed south and east into Bolivia and northern Chile — the two expansion patterns were geographically complementary, possibly by deliberate alliance and possibly by accident.

Other contemporary world powers like the Tang Chinese and the Byzantine Empire left detailed diplomatic correspondence and treaty records — Tiwanaku and Wari left nothing comparable, so the actual content of their interaction is invisible to modern reconstruction.

The single best window into the Wari-Tiwanaku relationship is Cerro Baúl in the Moquegua Valley, the only place in the entire Andes where the two empires lived within sight of each other — and even there the only documented event is a peaceful brewery shutdown ceremony.
subtitle: rivals, allies, or oblivious — three centuries of armed peaceful coexistence

title: Cerro Baúl
bullets: Cerro Baúl is a flat-topped mesa rising 2,000 feet above the Moquegua Valley with sheer cliffs on three sides — one of the most defensible sites in the central Andes and one of the most spectacularly impractical places anyone has ever chosen to build a city.

The Wari built a fortress city on the summit around 600 CE, in plain sight of Tiwanaku colonies on the valley floor below, and the two settlements coexisted within view of each other for roughly three centuries without intermarrying, fighting, or mixing culturally.

The summit fortress contained the largest known industrial brewery in the Middle Horizon, producing several hundred gallons of chicha per batch — far more than the few hundred Wari residents could possibly drink themselves.

Andean political authority was constituted through reciprocal feasting: the leader who hosted a large enough chicha feast became, by the act of hosting, the higher-status party in the relationship, and the Cerro Baúl brewery was essentially the embassy where this protocol was conducted.

Around 1000 CE, as the Wari empire collapsed, the elite at Cerro Baúl held a final feast, drank chicha from elaborately painted kero cups, smashed the cups against the brewery walls in a coordinated ceremony, and burned the building down before walking away.

Patrick Williams and Donna Nash reconstructed the entire closing ceremony from the position of the smashed cup fragments, the burning pattern, and the orderly arrangement of abandoned brewing equipment — nobody had looted the site for the next nine hundred years.

The smashing-the-cup ceremony was an act of decommissioning: the brewery would never produce diplomatic chicha again, the cups were destroyed to mark the end of that function, and the building was returned to the gods rather than left to the next political occupant.

What Cerro Baúl tells us about Wari-Tiwanaku relations is that the two empires invented something like modern peer-to-peer diplomatic relations — not alliance, not subordination, not conflict, just sustained ceremonial protocol between recognized equals.
subtitle: the brewery on the frontier and the smashing-cup ceremony that closed it

title: Wari Roads, Storehouses, Khipus
bullets: Wari built the first true paved road system in the central Andes, several thousand miles of integrated routes linking the capital to the planned provincial compounds — a network the Inca later inherited and refurbished as the famous Qhapaq Ñan.

Wari roads were designed for llama caravans and runners rather than wheeled vehicles, with stone staircases on the steepest sections, retaining walls on cliffs, and suspension bridges over gorges — the design that would still be in use a thousand years later.

Each Wari administrative compound contained rows of standardized qollqa storehouses holding charred grain, dried potato chuño, freeze-dried llama meat (charqui), bundled textiles, and copper ingots — imperial reserves rather than local food.
The Inca later scaled the storehouse model up enormously — Huánuco Pampa contained over 500 storage rooms, Cotapachi over 2,000 — but the basic system was Wari construction.

Wari khipus are the earliest known ancestors of the famous Inca knotted-string accounting devices, predating them by several centuries, with a different structural grammar of wrapped color-coded threads that modern scholars have not yet decoded.

Tocapu — the small geometric symbols woven into elite Wari tunics in dense grid patterns — may have been a proto-writing system that never quite developed past the proto stage, leaving the Andes as one of the only major civilizations to remain non-literate.

The leading hypothesis is that Wari is the historical reason Quechua became the dominant language family of the central Andes long before the Inca, spread by administrative imposition through the wamani system and adopted by the Inca centuries later.

The modern linguistic geography of the central Andes — Quechua across central Peru and Bolivia, Aymara around Lake Titicaca — is essentially the fossil imprint of the Middle Horizon empires, preserved through the Inca, the Spanish conquest, and the modern republics.
subtitle: the imperial infrastructure the Inca later inherited

title: Tiwanaku Religion & Iconography
bullets: The Staff God image — frontal figure holding paired staffs with a radiating headdress of feline and serpent figures — was preserved across two thousand years of Andean religion, from Chavín de Huántar around 500 BCE through Tiwanaku to the Inca creator god Wiracocha that the Spanish chroniclers described in the 1530s.

Two thousand years of unbroken iconographic transmission across multiple successor civilizations and the entire central Andean geography is unusual in any civilization, comparable in stability only to the longest-lived Egyptian deity images.

Both Tiwanaku and Wari elites consumed psychoactive plants in ritual contexts — vilca snuff blown through bone tubes, brews of the San Pedro cactus, coca leaves — to produce visionary religious experience interpreted as direct contact with the divine.

The 24-foot Bennett Monolith at Tiwanaku shows a frontal figure holding a kero drinking cup in one hand and a snuff tablet in the other — the ritual implements of chicha consumption and vilca inhalation, identified explicitly as the attributes of religious authority.

The animal triad of puma, condor, and snake encoded a vertical Andean cosmology: snake below in the underworld, puma in the middle living zone, condor above in the celestial — the same triadic system the Inca later inherited as puma, kuntur, and amaru.

The Inca city of Cusco was reportedly laid out in the shape of a giant puma with the fortress of Sacsayhuamán as its head — Middle Horizon iconographic vocabulary preserved and amplified centuries later.

Tiwanaku emphasized the celestial zone with its astronomical alignments and sun-disk iconography, while Wari emphasized the earth zone with its bounded administrative compounds and ushnu speaking platforms — variations on a shared religious framework.

The Inca religion the Spanish encountered in the sixteenth century — the Wiracocha-Inti-Pachamama theological system, the chicha-and-coca ritual culture, the mountain apus and lake huacas — was substantially the same religion Tiwanaku and Wari had been practicing six centuries earlier.
subtitle: the Staff God, vilca and San Pedro, and the puma-condor-snake triad

title: The Collapse
bullets: Both Tiwanaku and Wari collapsed almost simultaneously around 900–1100 CE, almost certainly because of a multi-decade drought documented independently in ice cores from the Quelccaya Ice Cap and sediment cores from the bottom of Lake Titicaca.

The drought drove Lake Titicaca's level down by 40 to 50 feet over decades, dropping the surrounding water table below the suka kollu raised-field channels and collapsing the agricultural system that fed Tiwanaku — the city de-populated slowly with no signs of conquest or violence.

Wari collapsed from the periphery inward as river flows declined and irrigation systems failed: peripheral compounds like Cerro Baúl and Pikillaqta were closed first with deliberate sealing rituals, then the capital itself was dismantled with the same orderly closure.

Both empires walked away from themselves rather than falling to invasion or revolt — the Wari elite made a series of orderly decisions to close the imperial infrastructure as climate conditions made it untenable.

What followed was the Late Intermediate Period of regional fragmentation, with the Chimú on the north coast building Chan Chan into the largest pre-Columbian city in South America, the Aymara kingdoms fighting over the altiplano, and the Killke chieftaincies coalescing in the Cusco basin.

When the Inca built their empire after 1400 CE they consciously rebuilt on the Wari template — the road system, the storehouses, the wamani provincial structure, the mit'a labor tribute, the ushnu platforms, the khipu accounting, the Quechua administrative language — all Middle Horizon inheritance.

When Francisco Pizarro marched up the Inca royal road from the coast to Cajamarca in 1532, he was walking on a roadbed that Wari engineers had laid down eight centuries earlier — the second iteration of an imperial template whose first run had ended in deliberate orderly closure.

The Maya Classic collapse, the Tang Dynasty collapse, and the Tiwanaku-Wari collapse all happened within roughly two centuries of each other on three different continents — the first millennium ended with imperial demolitions on a global scale, plausibly with shared climatic causation.
subtitle: drought, orderly imperial closure, and the Inca who built on Wari foundations
